questions.questionText: The normal GFR (Glomerular Filtration Rate) is approximately:
questions.explanation: Normal GFR ≈ 125 mL/min (180 L/day). Of this, ~99% is reabsorbed → urine output ~1-2 L/day. GFR is determined by Starling forces at the glomerulus: net filtration pressure = Pgc - Pbc - πgc. GFR decreases with age (~1 mL/min/year after 40). CKD stages based on GFR: Stage 1 (≥90), Stage 2 (60-89), Stage 3 (30-59), Stage 4 (15-29), Stage 5 (<15). Best measured by inulin clearance. Reference: Guyton — GFR.

questions.questionText: Inulin clearance is the gold standard for measuring GFR because inulin is:
questions.optionB: Freely filtered, neither reabsorbed nor secreted
questions.explanation: Inulin: freely filtered at the glomerulus, NOT reabsorbed, NOT secreted, NOT metabolized, NOT protein-bound → clearance of inulin = GFR exactly. Clinically, creatinine clearance is used (slight overestimation because creatinine is slightly secreted in PCT). PAH clearance estimates RPF (renal plasma flow) — PAH is filtered AND actively secreted (almost completely cleared in one pass). Reference: Guyton — Clearance.

questions.questionText: The juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA) consists of:
questions.optionB: JG cells (in afferent arteriole), macula densa (in DCT), and extraglomerular mesangial cells
questions.explanation: JGA components: (1) JG cells (modified smooth muscle cells in wall of afferent arteriole — produce and release renin), (2) Macula densa (specialized epithelial cells in early DCT — sense NaCl concentration, regulate renin release via tubuloglomerular feedback), (3) Extraglomerular mesangial cells (Lacis cells). JGA regulates GFR and renin secretion. Low NaCl at macula densa → renin release → RAAS activation. Reference: Guyton — JGA.

questions.questionText: The maximum reabsorption of water and solutes occurs in the:
questions.explanation: PCT reabsorbs: ~65-67% of filtered Na+, water, Cl-, K+, HCO3-, ~100% glucose and amino acids, ~90% bicarbonate. PCT is the workhorse of reabsorption (obligatory, not regulated by hormones). Loop of Henle: ~25% Na+/Cl-. DCT/collecting duct: fine-tuning under hormonal control (aldosterone, ADH). PCT has brush border (microvilli) for increased surface area. Reference: Guyton — Tubular Reabsorption.

questions.questionText: ADH (antidiuretic hormone/vasopressin) acts on which part of the nephron?
questions.explanation: ADH (from posterior pituitary) acts on V2 receptors in the collecting duct → cAMP → insertion of aquaporin-2 (AQP2) water channels in the apical membrane → increased water reabsorption → concentrated urine. Without ADH: dilute urine (diabetes insipidus). Central DI: no ADH production. Nephrogenic DI: kidneys don't respond to ADH. ADH also causes vasoconstriction via V1 receptors. Reference: Guyton — ADH Action.

questions.questionText: Aldosterone acts on the kidney to:
questions.optionB: Increase Na+ reabsorption and K+/H+ secretion in the principal cells of collecting duct
questions.explanation: Aldosterone (from adrenal zona glomerulosa) acts on principal cells of the cortical collecting duct: increases ENaC (epithelial Na+ channels) and Na+/K+ ATPase → Na+ reabsorption ↑, K+ secretion ↑, H+ secretion ↑. Stimulated by: angiotensin II, hyperkalemia, ACTH. Inhibited by ANP. Conn syndrome (primary hyperaldosteronism): hypertension, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis. Spironolactone is an aldosterone antagonist. Reference: Guyton — Aldosterone.

questions.questionText: The countercurrent multiplication system in the loop of Henle creates a medullary osmotic gradient. The maximum osmolality at the tip of the loop is approximately:
questions.explanation: Countercurrent multiplication: single effect of NaCl transport in the thick ascending limb (TAL, impermeable to water) × multiplication by counter-flow → creates a corticomedullary osmotic gradient (300 mOsm cortex → 1200 mOsm inner medulla). Urea recycling also contributes to medullary hypertonicity. Vasa recta (countercurrent exchange) preserves the gradient. Maximum urine concentration ≈ 1200 mOsm/kg (with maximal ADH). Reference: Guyton — Countercurrent Mechanism.

questions.questionText: The thick ascending limb of the Loop of Henle is:
questions.optionA: Permeable to water and impermeable to NaCl
questions.optionB: Impermeable to water but actively transports NaCl (via NKCC2)
questions.optionC: Permeable to both water and NaCl
questions.explanation: TAL (diluting segment): IMPERMEABLE to water, actively reabsorbs NaCl via NKCC2 (Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter) — target of loop diuretics (furosemide). This makes tubular fluid DILUTE (hypo-osmotic) as it enters the DCT. The removed NaCl makes the medullary interstitium HYPERTONIC. Thin descending limb: permeable to water, impermeable to NaCl (water moves out by osmosis). Reference: Guyton — Loop of Henle.

questions.questionText: Renal clearance of a substance equals GFR when the substance is:
questions.optionC: Only filtered, neither reabsorbed nor secreted
questions.explanation: If a substance is freely filtered and NOT reabsorbed or secreted, its clearance = GFR (e.g., inulin, approximately creatinine). If reabsorbed: clearance < GFR (e.g., glucose clearance = 0 below Tm, urea, Na+). If secreted: clearance > GFR (e.g., PAH, creatinine slightly). If clearance > GFR → net secretion. If clearance < GFR → net reabsorption. Reference: Guyton — Renal Clearance.

questions.questionText: ANP (Atrial Natriuretic Peptide) causes:
questions.optionB: Vasodilation, natriuresis (Na+ excretion), and decreased blood volume
questions.explanation: ANP is released from atrial myocytes in response to atrial stretch (volume expansion). Effects: (1) Vasodilation (decreases BP), (2) Natriuresis and diuresis (inhibits Na+ reabsorption in collecting duct), (3) Inhibits renin and aldosterone secretion, (4) Increases GFR (dilates afferent, constricts efferent arteriole). BNP (from ventricles) has similar actions. Both are guanylyl cyclase receptor ligands → cGMP. Reference: Guyton — ANP.

questions.questionText: The tubuloglomerular feedback mechanism involves:
questions.optionA: Macula densa sensing NaCl concentration and adjusting afferent arteriolar tone
questions.explanation: TGF: increased NaCl delivery to macula densa (due to increased GFR) → adenosine/ATP release → afferent arteriolar constriction → GFR decreases (negative feedback). Decreased NaCl → afferent dilation → GFR increases. Also stimulates renin release from JG cells. This autoregulatory mechanism stabilizes GFR and NaCl delivery. Works in coordination with the myogenic mechanism. Reference: Guyton — TGF.

questions.questionText: In metabolic acidosis, the kidney compensates by:
questions.optionB: Increasing H+ secretion, NH4+ excretion, and HCO3- reabsorption (generating new HCO3-)
questions.explanation: Renal compensation for metabolic acidosis: (1) Increased H+ secretion (PCT, collecting duct intercalated cells), (2) Increased NH4+ excretion (glutamine metabolism in PCT → NH4+ + new HCO3-), (3) Complete reabsorption of filtered HCO3-, (4) Increased titratable acidity (H+ buffered by phosphate → H2PO4-). Takes 3-5 days for full compensation. Respiratory compensation (hyperventilation to lower PCO2) is much faster. Reference: Guyton — Renal Acid-Base.

questions.questionText: The renal threshold for glucose is approximately:
questions.explanation: Renal threshold ≈ 180 mg/dL (blood glucose above which glucose appears in urine — glycosuria). Below this, all filtered glucose is reabsorbed by SGLT2 (90%) and SGLT1 (10%). Tubular maximum for glucose (TmG) ≈ 375 mg/min (complete saturation of all transporters). Splay: glucose appears before theoretical Tm due to heterogeneity of nephrons. In diabetes, persistent hyperglycemia exceeds threshold → glycosuria. Reference: Guyton — Glucose Reabsorption.

questions.questionText: Free water clearance (CH2O) is positive when producing:
questions.explanation: CH2O = V - Cosm (urine flow rate - osmolar clearance). Positive CH2O: kidneys excreting 'free water' → dilute/hypotonic urine (water diuresis, no ADH). Negative CH2O (TCH2O): kidneys retaining free water → concentrated/hypertonic urine (ADH present). Zero CH2O: isosmotic urine (osmolality = plasma). Normal kidney can dilute to ~50 mOsm and concentrate to ~1200 mOsm. Reference: Guyton — Free Water Clearance.

questions.questionText: The Filtration Fraction (FF) is defined as:
questions.explanation: FF = GFR/RPF. Normal: 125/625 ≈ 0.20 (20% of plasma is filtered). RPF ≈ 625 mL/min (measured by PAH clearance). RBF ≈ 1100 mL/min. Increased FF: efferent constriction (angiotensin II) → GFR maintained but RPF decreases → more protein-free filtrate → increased peritubular oncotic pressure → increased PCT reabsorption. Decreased FF: afferent constriction (equally reduces GFR and RPF). Reference: Guyton — Filtration Fraction.

questions.questionText: Loop diuretics (furosemide) act by inhibiting:
questions.optionB: NKCC2 (Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter) in the thick ascending limb
questions.explanation: Furosemide inhibits NKCC2 in the TAL → blocks NaCl reabsorption → natriuresis, diuresis, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, hypocalcemia. Most potent diuretics ('high-ceiling'). Thiazides: inhibit NCC (Na+/Cl- cotransporter) in DCT → hypocalciuria (used in Ca2+ stones). K+-sparing: spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist), amiloride/triamterene (block ENaC). Acetazolamide: carbonic anhydrase inhibitor in PCT. Reference: Guyton — Diuretics.

questions.questionText: The clearance of PAH (para-aminohippuric acid) estimates:
questions.explanation: PAH is freely filtered AND actively secreted by the PCT → almost completely cleared from plasma in a single pass (extraction ratio ~0.9). PAH clearance = ERPF ≈ 625 mL/min. True RPF = ERPF/extraction ratio. RBF = RPF/(1-Hematocrit). PAH has a tubular maximum (TmPAH) — above which secretory mechanism saturates. At low plasma concentrations, PAH clearance ≈ RPF. Reference: Guyton — PAH Clearance.

questions.questionText: In diabetes insipidus, urine is:
questions.explanation: Diabetes insipidus: insufficient ADH (central DI — hypothalamic/pituitary lesion) or renal resistance to ADH (nephrogenic DI — lithium, hypercalcemia). Result: inability to concentrate urine → large volumes of dilute urine (polyuria, polydipsia). Central DI responds to desmopressin (DDAVP). Nephrogenic DI does not. Water deprivation test differentiates: central DI concentrates with DDAVP, nephrogenic doesn't. Reference: Guyton — Diabetes Insipidus.

questions.questionText: The afferent arteriole constriction causes:
questions.explanation: Afferent arteriolar constriction: decreased glomerular capillary pressure → decreased GFR AND decreased RPF. Filtration fraction may be unchanged. Efferent arteriolar constriction: increased glomerular pressure → GFR maintained or slightly increased, RPF decreases → FF INCREASES. NSAIDs (→ afferent) and ACE inhibitors (→ efferent dilation) both reduce GFR in certain conditions. Reference: Guyton — Arteriolar Effects on GFR.

questions.questionText: The most abundant solute contributing to the medullary osmotic gradient is:
questions.explanation: Medullary hypertonicity: NaCl (mainly from TAL NKCC2 transport) accounts for ~50% and urea (recycled between collecting duct and thin ascending limb via UT-A1/A2 urea transporters) accounts for ~50%. Urea recycling is ADH-dependent (ADH increases urea permeability in inner medullary collecting duct). The gradient rises from 300 mOsm at the cortex to 1200 mOsm at the papillary tip. Reference: Guyton — Medullary Gradient.

questions.questionText: Potassium balance is primarily regulated by the kidney at the level of the:
questions.optionB: Principal cells of the cortical collecting duct
questions.explanation: K+ secretion in principal cells of CCD is the primary regulated step. Factors increasing K+ secretion: aldosterone (main), high K+ intake, increased tubular flow, alkalosis. Aldosterone stimulates ENaC → Na+ entry → lumen negative → K+ secretion through ROMK channels. PCT reabsorbs ~65% K+ (paracellular), TAL ~25%. H+ and K+ compete for secretion → alkalosis promotes K+ secretion (hypokalemia), acidosis inhibits it. Reference: Guyton — K+ Regulation.

questions.questionText: The vasa recta function as countercurrent exchangers to:
questions.optionB: Preserve the medullary osmotic gradient while supplying blood to the medulla
questions.explanation: Vasa recta (hairpin-shaped peritubular capillaries in the medulla) act as countercurrent EXCHANGERS (passive): descending limb equilibrates with hypertonic medulla (gains solute, loses water), ascending limb does the reverse. This prevents washout of the medullary gradient. Countercurrent MULTIPLICATION (loop of Henle) creates the gradient; vasa recta preserve it. High medullary blood flow would wash out the gradient. Reference: Guyton — Vasa Recta.

questions.questionText: Autoregulation of renal blood flow maintains constant GFR between MAP of:
questions.explanation: Renal autoregulation: maintains constant RBF and GFR over MAP 80-180 mmHg. Two mechanisms: (1) Myogenic response (Bayliss effect — afferent arteriole constricts in response to increased pressure), (2) Tubuloglomerular feedback (TGF — macula densa). Below MAP ~80 mmHg, autoregulation fails → GFR drops. Above ~180 mmHg, autoregulation overwhelmed. Important: ACE inhibitors reduce GFR in bilateral renal artery stenosis. Reference: Guyton — Renal Autoregulation.
